query: cell death mechanism in mesial temporal sclerosis

retrieved_docs.title: Mesial Temporal Lobe Epilepsy -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Hippocampal sclerosis is histopathologically seen as segmental pyramidal cell loss in CA1, CA3, and CA4 regions, whereas CA2 pyramidal and dentate gyrus granule cells are most seizure resistant. [14] Neuronal cell loss is associated with reactive astrogliosis causing tissue stiffening, which has been traditionally termed as "Ammon's horn sclerosis." [15] Some of the proposed pathomechanisms include disruption of neuronal circuitries, causing aberrant mossy fiber sprouting and molecular rearrangement/plasticity of ion channel and neurotransmitter receptor expression. [16] [17] Abnormalities have also been noted in the dentate gyrus in the form of granule cell dispersion. [18] Additionally, variable cell loss is also detectable in adjacent cortical regions, including the subiculum, entorhinal cortex, and amygdala.

retrieved_docs.title: Focal Impaired Awareness Seizure -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most common pathological feature of temporal lobe epilepsy is hippocampal sclerosis. Hippocampal sclerosis (HS) consists of neuronal loss and gliosis in the dentate nucleus and pyramidal layer of the hippocampus. There is controversy about whether HS is a result of seizures or a cause of seizures. The mechanism of damage in HS is glutamate discharge during the seizure episode. The most frequent site of damage is in the CA1 area of the hippocampus.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporal Seizure -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Being the earliest discovered, and most common pathological finding on the autopsy of patients with temporal lobe epilepsy, [11] hippocampal sclerosis has been the most examined to date. As the name suggests, the primary pathology in this condition occurs in the hippocampus, with neuronal loss, or atrophy in the hilar regions, predominantly localized to the CA1, CA3, C4, and dentate gyrus, with CA2 sparing. Expansion of the normally dense granule cell layer, mossy fiber sprouting, and gliosis being some common findings on pathological specimens. It should be noted, however, that hippocampal sclerosis has a variety of pathological subtypes. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Partial Epilepsy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mesial temporal lobe epilepsies (MTLE) are identified as the most common form of partial epilepsy. The etiology of MTLE is linked to hippocampal sclerosis. Hippocampal sclerosis is caused by the disruption of normal mRNA transcription leading to various malfunctions of healthy cells and ultimately resulting in permanent sclerotic changes. [8] As the sclerotic tissue replaces healthy functioning tissue, the patient develops resistance to anti-epileptic medications resulting in drug-resistant epilepsy.

retrieved_docs.title: Mesial Temporal Lobe Epilepsy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Most cases of MTLE are sporadic in occurrence, although familial forms are not uncommon. [5] One study showed that as high as one-fifth of the newly diagnosed non-lesional MTLE could have a familial attribute. Research has identified a genetic locus for familial MTLE in a large family with autosomal dominant MTLE phenotype. [6] The familial MTLE cases have been shown to exhibit a complex inheritance pattern and usually do not exhibit mesial temporal sclerosis on imaging. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: White Matter Lesions -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Traumatic axonal injury refers to severe axonal mechanical damage due to a rotational acceleration of the brain. Although its pathophysiology is complicated, the injury damage is firstly due to a mechanical break involving axonal microtubules. This stretch induces axonal damage through undulations, breaks, and direct membrane mechanoporation with calcium influx. This mechanism leads to the activation of several injurious pathways, including caspase-mediated proteolysis and cytokine-mediated microglia recruitment with impairment of axonal transport and the aggregation of transported proteins in varicose swellings. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Mesial Temporal Lobe Epilepsy -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Several classification systems have been proposed for HS. The most widely used is the ILAE classification system, which divides HS into three types based on a semi-quantitative survey of segmental cell loss within hippocampal subfields. International League Against Epilepsy (ILAE) type 1 has both CA1 and CA4 loss; ILAE Type 2 has predominant CA1 loss, and ILAE Type 3 has predominant CA4 loss.

retrieved_docs.title: Corticobasal Degeneration -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Additional genome-wide association study results have implicated myelin-associated oligodendrocyte basic protein and son of sevenless homolog 1, highlighting a potential role for white matter pathology and non-tau-mediated mechanisms. There is also emerging evidence of genetic overlap between CBD and immune-mediated diseases, suggesting a possible role for immune system dysregulation. Despite these advances, the precise triggers of tauopathy and the full extent of pathogenic pathways in CBD remain incompletely understood, underscoring the need for further research to facilitate targeted therapeutic development.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Axon -- Pathophysiology -- Demyelination
retrieved_docs.content: Demyelination is a destructive removal of myelin that induces a paramount impairment in impulse transmission. A demyelinated axon, indeed, transmits impulses up to 10 times slower than normal myelinated, whereas a complete stop of the transmission is also possible. Myelin and axonal degradation are observed in multiple sclerosis (MS). In this immune-mediated inflammatory disease, the inflammatory and demyelinating plaques are accompanied by perivenular macrophage, lymphocyte infiltration, and oligodendrocyte degradation. The observable clinical symptoms associated with the latter depend on the location of the affected neurons. The exponential downward trend of neurodegeneration development is complicated and could be related to the dysfunction of ion channels, calcium overload, synaptopathy, activation of apoptotic pathways, or glutamate-related excitotoxicity. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Temporal Seizure -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although about 10 to 15 percent of children who develop febrile seizures progress to a diagnosis of epilepsy,  there is no compelling evidence of causality between a history of febrile seizures and the development of temporal lobe epilepsy. What is evident, however, is the association of lesions in anatomical correlates on neuroimaging, such as mesial temporal lobe sclerosis, in the pediatric population, and the development of temporal lobe epilepsy. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Frontotemporal Lobe Dementia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Frontotemporal dementia has three distinct clinical syndromes based on the underlying pathologic mechanism characterized by intracellular deposition of abnormal proteins aggregates in the frontal and temporal lobes resulting in the degeneration of neurons, microvacuoles formation, and astrocytosis; hence the term frontotemporal lobar degeneration. Abnormal aggregates of transactive DNA-binding protein TDP-43 account for 50% of cases, microtubules-associated tau protein for 45%, and in 5% to 10% cases, tumor-associated protein fused in sarcoma has been found. [5] The abnormal tau protein aggregates have been identified as the main culprit in patients with both sporadic and familial types of FTD except in the svPPA, in which TDP-43 aggregates predominate. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Stuttering (Stammering) -- Etiology -- Acquired Stuttering
retrieved_docs.content: Neurogenic stuttering: Neurogenic stuttering arises from damage to specific brain tissue regions due to an insult. [2] Potential causes of such damage or injury include stroke (the most prevalent), significant traumatic brain injury, hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy, various dementing diseases, Parkinson's disease, sequelae of dialysis, corticobasal ganglionic degeneration, multiple sclerosis, and epilepsy. [2] [11] [12] [13] [14] [15] Consistent with various injury mechanisms, neurogenic stuttering exhibits variability in its underlying neural substrates and does not distinctly localize to a specific brain region. Commonly affected areas include the bilateral hemispheres, subcortical white matter tracts, the cerebellum, and deep nuclei such as the basal ganglia, thalamus, and brainstem. Notably, the left hemisphere, typically dominant for language, is more commonly affected than the right hemisphere. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Temporal Lobe -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Temporal lobe lesions can be categorized into 3 based on their MRI features. Unilateral volume loss Mesial temporal sclerosis Old infarction Remote brain injury Prior surgery Bilateral pathology Alzheimer disease Fronto-temporal dementia Age-related volume loss Mesial temporal sclerosis (10%) Limbic encephalitis Postradiation changes Space-occupying lesions Cystic neoplasia Solid neoplasm Encephalitis Abscess Vascular malformations Acute infarction Acute trauma [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Paraneoplastic Limbic Encephalitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In this form of LE, the pathological process is primarily mediated by a predominant T-cell mechanism, as evidenced by cytotoxic T cells in pathological specimens. The antibodies are not directly pathogenic but are markers for concurrent cytotoxic T-cell-mediated autoimmunity. Neuronal tissues share similar antigenicity with intracellular tumor antigens, contributing to neurological manifestations. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Temporal Seizure -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Epilepsy affects about seventy million of the global population and is a chronic, debilitating condition, [1] with the vast majority of these cases originating in the temporal lobe. Furthermore, epilepsy of the temporal lobe can further subdivide into two categories based on the anatomical origin of epileptic focus: Mesial Temporal Lobe Epilepsy (MTLE): Involving the anatomy of the innermost structure of the temporal lobe, including the hippocampus, parahippocampal gyrus, and amygdala; this is the most common form of temporal lobe seizures and is usually secondary to a pathological process known as hippocampal sclerosis (HS). Lateral Temporal Lobe Epilepsy (LTLE): Also referred to as neocortical temporal lobe seizures.  These are very rare and most commonly secondary to genetic or acquired structural/anatomical lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporal Seizure -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: As noted, temporal lobe epilepsies arise through a myriad of etiologies, regardless of the focus of epileptogenic activity. For simplicity, the lateral temporal lobe epilepsies fall into categories as either lesional or non-lesional. Lesional causes are often secondary to anatomical aberrancies. However, the lateral temporal lobe epilepsies have been a poorly studied group to date. Recent studies reveal a significant number of cases clustered in families or secondary to idiopathic genetic mutations. [13] There has also been evidence regarding the role of astrocytes in the pathogenesis of epilepsy as well as interconnected epileptic networks. [14] [15] [16] [17]

The clinical course descriptor “Transitory” is applied to transient cerebral ischemia, which corresponds to a transient ischemic attack (نوبة إقفارية عابرة), highlighting the temporary nature of some ischemic events. Overall, occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries is a pivotal pathological mechanism that can lead to multiple forms of cerebral infarction, may be asymptomatic, is associated with intracranial arterial sclerosis, and is integrated within a structured classification of cerebrovascular circulatory diseases. These relationships inform diagnosis, risk assessment, therapeutic decision‑making (including endarterectomy), and the broader understanding of disease hierarchy in patient care.

Collectively, these relationships demonstrate how various cerebrovascular conditions—ranging from arterial sclerosis and hypertension‑related encephalopathy to arterial dissection, multiple aneurysms, and transient ischemic attacks—are organized within hierarchical disease categories, linked by clinical courses, and associated with related vascular abnormalities, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing cerebrovascular health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 162
retrieved_docs.content: indicating that not all occlusions inevitably produce infarcts. Structural evaluation of intracranial arteries (Structure of intracranial artery) identifies تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial arterial sclerosis) as a finding site, which is itself derived from precerebral arterial occlusion/stenosis, highlighting the pathophysiological link between arterial wall disease and luminal narrowing. These intracranial vessels may serve as indirect targets for endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck, a procedure classified under استئصال باطنة الشريان (endarterectomy). استئصال باطنة الشريان is listed as a member of حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ), reflecting its therapeutic role in managing thrombotic and embolic arterial conditions. Overall, the network emphasizes that precerebral arterial occlusion or stenosis—whether symptomatic, asymptomatic, thrombotic, embolic, or associated with intracranial arterial sclerosis—is the central pathogenic substrate that leads to cerebral (and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 177
retrieved_docs.content: is the inverse‑ISA of arterial occlusion (انسداد الشرايين) and connects to basilar artery stenosis (تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي) and basilar artery thrombosis (خثار الشريان القاعدي). Embolism of the basilar artery (Embolie; arteria basilaris) also maps to this pathway and is the inverse‑ISA of cerebral artery embolism (انصمام الشريان المخي). Other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular disease is further linked to cerebral arterial sclerosis, which in turn relates to multiple saccular aneurysms (خَرَف احتشائي متعدّد). Collectively, these relationships illustrate how embolic, thrombotic, and occlusive processes in precerebral and basilar arteries lead to brain infarction, how such events fit within the taxonomy of cerebrovascular and systemic circulatory disorders, and how associated conditions such as arterial sclerosis, hypertension‑induced brain injury, and chronic rheumatic heart disease intersect in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 188
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a spectrum of cerebrovascular disorders classified under “Circulatory Diseases and Syndromes of Nervous System.” Cerebral infarction (الاحتشاء) results from vascular occlusion (انسداد الأوعية الدموية) of precerebral arteries, whether caused by embolism, thrombosis, or basilar‑artery occlusion/stenosis. Occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries (تصلب عصيدي في المخ) is a form of arterial narrowing (تضيق الشرايين) that may be asymptomatic, producing only transient systemic signs such as syncope (غشي) and affecting general wellbeing. These occlusive processes are linked to chronic arterial changes such as cerebral arterial sclerosis (تصلب الشرايين المخية) and intracranial arterial sclerosis, which in turn predispose to acute cerebrovascular accidents (حادثة وعائية دماغية), a child of transient cerebral ischemia (نوبة إقفارية عابرة) and a member of the SMQ “أمراض وعائية إقْفارِيّة في الجهاز العصبي المركزي.” Hypertension‑related brain damage (اعتلال دماغي ناجم عن فَرْط ضَغْطِ الدَّم) is included among other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular diseases (Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten). Anatomically, the structure of the intracranial artery serves as an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy (استئصال باطنة الشريان) of head‑and‑neck vessels, a surgical intervention that addresses cases of arterial thrombosis and embolism (حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية). Specific entities such as basilar‑artery narrowing (تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي), basilar‑artery thrombosis (خثار الشريان القاعدي), and basilar‑artery embolism (Embolie; arteria basilaris) are mapped to the same occlusion/stenosis category and are inverse‑isa of cerebral artery embolism (انصمام الشريان المخي). The vascular system is also the anatomic site for renal vessel disorders (اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية) and arterial ulceration (تسلخ الشرايين). Cerebral arterial sclerosis is associated with multiple sclerotic atrophic changes (خَرَف احتشائي متعدّد).

Together, these interconnections emphasize the centrality of the brain in diverse pathological, diagnostic, and therapeutic contexts, and they underscore the importance of recognizing overlapping disease categories for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: Other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease is mapped to تصلب الشرايين المخية (cerebral arterial sclerosis), and تصلب الشرايين المخية has a relationship with خَرَف احتشائي متعدّد (multiple lacunar infarcts). This indicates that cerebral arterial sclerosis is classified within the broader category of other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular disease and is associated with the development of multiple lacunar strokes, underscoring its relevance in the assessment and management of small‑vessel cerebrovascular pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 949
retrieved_docs.content: Central nervous system vascular diseases (SMQ) are classified as a subset of cerebrovascular accidents (حادثة وعائية دماغية). Cerebral artery sclerosis (تصلب الشرايين المخية) is closely linked to cerebrovascular accidents, serving as a contributing factor to stroke. In addition, cerebral artery sclerosis is associated with the development of multiple lacunar infarcts (خَرَف احتشائي متعدّد). These interconnections underscore the importance of arterial sclerosis in the pathogenesis of various cerebrovascular events and highlight its relevance for diagnosis, risk assessment, and management in patient care.

An unspecified paralytic syndrome (Paralytisch syndroom, niet gespecificeerd) shares a broader relationship with unspecified brain disorders, indicating that motor deficits may arise from diverse, not yet fully defined cerebral lesions. Acute cerebrovascular disease is treated as a specific manifestation within the umbrella of persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction, reinforcing the link between acute vascular events and migraine‑related infarction. Collectively, these concepts demonstrate how neuroanatomical structures, cognitive and emotional functions, imaging

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: can reveal periventricular cysts (brain cysts, كيسة دماغية), enlarged Sylvian fissure, encephalitis, metabolic brain disease (اعتلال دماغي أيضي), brain compression, cerebral edema (أوديما دماغية), traumatic brain edema (a subtype of general brain edema), and other unspecified brain disorders (اضطراب دماغي غير محدد). The brain is the locus of higher cognitive functions such as thinking (myšlení) and emotional experiences like guilt (الشعور بالذنب), and it is the site of diverse pathologies including toxic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي سمي), arthropod‑transmitted viral encephalitis (التهاب الدماغ الفيروسي المنقول بالمفصليات), and acute brain injury (إصابة حادة في الدماغ), which is a specific form of cerebral pathology (اعتلال دماغي). Vascular and cerebrovascular disorders intersect with these entities: cerebral artery sclerosis (تصلب الشرايين المخية) belongs to the physical‑psychosomatic subcluster and contributes to multiple lacunar infarcts (خَرَف احتشائي متعدّد); migraine with

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: The brain (mozek) serves as the anatomical site for a wide spectrum of neurological findings, diseases, and therapeutic interventions. Imaging studies such as a CT scan of the head revealing a periventricular cyst are classified as brain cysts (كيسة دماغية), and a CT scan showing an enlarged Sylvian fissure is considered an unspecified brain disorder (اضطراب دماغي غير محدد). Pathological processes identified in the brain include metabolic brain disease (اعتلال دماغي أيضي), brain atrophy (انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ), toxic brain disease (اعتلال دماغي سمي), and cerebral edema (أوديما دماغية). Traumatic brain edema (Traumatisch hersenoedeem) is a subtype of cerebral edema, while acute brain injury (إصابة حادة في الدماغ) is a form of brain disease (اعتلال دماغي). Encephalitis is a condition that occurs within the brain, and acute cerebrovascular disease is linked to persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction, a specific type of migraine with ischemic complication. This migraine variant is also categorized under unspecified brain disorders, highlighting its potential to produce ischemic brain injury. An unspecified paralytic syndrome (Paralytisch syndroom, niet gespecificeerd) is broadly related to unspecified brain disorders, underscoring the overlap between motor deficits and diffuse cerebral pathology. Beyond structural and vascular abnormalities, the brain is the locus of essential biological processes such as thinking (myšlení) and emotional experiences like guilt (الشعور بالذنب). These functional aspects are directly targeted by therapeutic procedures, for example, electrocoagulation therapy (العلاج بالتخليج الكهربي) performed on brain tissue. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how imaging findings, diverse neuropathologies, functional processes, and interventions converge on the brain, emphasizing the importance of integrated assessment and management in neurological patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 157
retrieved_docs.content: emphasizing its role as a specific cause of cerebral edema. Finally, إصابة حادة في الدماغ (acute brain injury) is considered a form of اعتلال دماغي, reinforcing the concept that acute traumatic or ischemic insults to the mozek constitute a type of brain disease. Together, these relationships illustrate how various imaging findings, inflammatory, metabolic, vascular, toxic, and traumatic processes converge on the mozek, linking structural abnormalities, physiological functions, and clinical syndromes within an integrated framework of brain health and disease.
